title: Identifying common chicken ailments
subtitle: A practical reference for the most frequent health problems in backyard flocks.
excerpt: Most chicken health problems have visible signs that appear before the bird becomes seriously ill. Knowing what to look for during daily checks lets you catch issues early, when they are still manageable.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018). RSPCA guidance on common poultry diseases. Merck Veterinary Manual, poultry section.

glossaryTerms.definition: A condition in which food ferments in the crop rather than passing normally into the stomach. The crop feels squishy rather than firm, and the bird may have a sour smell from the beak. Caused by candida overgrowth, usually following antibiotic use or access to mouldy feed.
A blockage of the crop, usually from long dry grass or fibrous material that cannot break down. The crop feels hard and does not empty overnight. Unlike sour crop, there is no fermentation smell.

A condition in which a hen cannot pass a fully formed egg. Signs include straining in a penguin stance, a swollen abdomen below the vent, and loss of interest in food. Requires prompt attention; a retained egg can cause peritonitis within 24 to 48 hours.

content.text: A healthy hen is active, alert, and eating. Changes in posture, droppings, feather condition, or appetite are the first signals that something is wrong. This guide covers the ailments you are most likely to encounter in a small backyard flock.

Feel the crop (the bulge at the base of the neck) first thing in the morning before the hen has eaten. It should be flat or nearly so; the overnight fast should have emptied it. A crop that is still full in the morning points to either a
(soft and squidgy, sour smell at the beak) or an
(firm, doughy, no smell). Isolate the bird, withdraw food but not water, and monitor for 24 hours. If the crop has not reduced in size by the next morning, see a vet.

Open-mouthed breathing, rattling or gurgling sounds, nasal discharge, and swollen sinuses below the eyes are all respiratory warning signs. In a flock context, respiratory disease spreads quickly. Isolate any bird showing these signs immediately and contact a vet; several bacterial and viral infections present similarly and require different treatment.

problem stands in a wide-legged, upright position and strains repeatedly. You may feel a hard mass when you gently palpate the abdomen below the vent. Warm baths (10 to 15 minutes in shallow warm water) can help the hen relax enough to pass the egg. If this does not resolve the situation within a couple of hours, treat it as a vet emergency.

Loose droppings can be normal. Cecal droppings (a brown, paste-like discharge produced once or twice a day) are normal and should not be confused with illness. True diarrhoea is watery, frequent, and often green or yellow. Single birds with loose droppings that are otherwise eating normally often recover without intervention. A bird that is hunched, not eating, or whose droppings are bloody needs a vet.

Feather loss on the back and saddle is usually caused by a cockerel's mating activity or feather pecking by flock-mates. Feather loss starting at the neck and moving down the body in late summer is a normal moult. Red, irritated skin, or birds pecking at themselves repeatedly, suggests external parasites; check under the wings and around the vent for red mite, lice, or their egg clusters at the base of feathers.

Any bird that is not eating, is hunched with fluffed feathers for more than 12 hours, has discharge from the eyes or nares, shows neurological signs (head tilt, circling, falling over), or has been attacked by a predator should see a vet. Poultry medicine is a specialist area; not all vets treat chickens, so identify your nearest avian or farm vet before you have an emergency.
